informal: Prove that there exist infinitely many integers $n$ such that $n, n+1, n+2$ are each the sum of the squares of two integers.
reasoning: The Lean encoding "∀ N, ∃ n > N, P(n)" is the standard way to express "there exist infinitely many n satisfying P". Although the informal statement says "integers" while Lean uses ℕ, any integer that is a sum of two integer squares is necessarily non-negative, so the set of relevant n coincides with a subset of ℕ. Likewise, an integer m is a sum of two integer squares iff it is a sum of two natural-number squares (take absolute values), so writing the squares with i : Fin 6 → ℕ does not change the assertion. The three conditions n = a²+b², n+1 = c²+d², n+2 = e²+f² match exactly the informal requirement that n, n+1, n+2 are each sums of two squares. Therefore the Lean signature is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
